what_it_does: This file is a design and build-out plan for the node mechanism so it can serve both the operable app surface and the concurrent-cognition layer; it maps the current registry-driven state, lists prioritized build items, and analyzes where the two uses share the substrate versus where they must diverge.
observations: Maps the current node mechanism through a registry-driven lens with a layer-by-layer gap table.
Prioritizes B1 parallel dispatch (wave-synchronous) and B2 request-concurrency budget as the top shared build items.
Identifies ROLE_REGISTRY as a hardcoded single-role dict inside suite.py and frames its generalization as the master registry-driven gap.
States that the Edge contract currently has no kind or resolve field; branching and fan-in are implemented as nodes, not edge types.
Part D makes the shared-vs-divergent seam analysis the centerpiece, listing shared mechanisms and divergent axes side by side.
Proposes a proving spike combining parallel dispatch, request budget, json_schema transport, a 2-role registry, and a resolve node.
Distinguishes Observed claims from Inferred claims and flags contract-touching items with CONFIRM.
standouts: Wave-synchronous parallel dispatch design that keeps store writes single-threaded while parallelizing read-only run() calls.
Role registry framed as the master registry-driven gap with three explicit options (node-type, data registry, llm instance + descriptor).
Edge-type analysis concluding that dynamic injection/resolve is the only genuinely net-new edge.
Shared lower substrate plus two thin drivers architecture: app Suite.run versus cognition _run_swarm.
conventions: Uses labeled parts (A-E) and a single prioritized build-out list at the end.
References prior docs rather than re-deriving their file:line evidence.
Tags each build item with [SHARED], [COGNITION], or [APP].
Distinguishes Observed from Inferred claims explicitly.
concerns: Many recommendations are labeled tentative and require confirmation, leaving the design unresolved in this file.
Multiple items flagged CONFIRM imply contract widening that is not yet decided.
Cross-run store-safety arguments rely on assumptions about content determinism that are not executed or verified here.
notes: A build-out plan that turns the existing node mechanism into a dual-use substrate for app and cognition, with the shared-vs-divergent seam as the central design question.
questions: What exact criteria will decide between the three role-registry options?
How will the N and R values for the request-concurrency semaphore be chosen at runtime?
What is the precise schema and cost model for a resolve edge or resolve node?
Which addressing namespace will be used for cognition outputs: run://<turn>/<role> or a new swarm:// namespace?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the file states it is a companion to the reuse map focused on build-out and seam analysis, and it delivers that.
apparent_themes: Separation of shared mechanism from use-specific driver
intention_signals: It is trying to plan the build-out that hardens the existing node mechanism into a shared substrate for both the operator app and an internal concurrent-cognition swarm, while making explicit where the two uses must remain separate.
novelty: It treats the node mechanism as intentionally dual-use from the start and organizes the entire design around a shared-vs-divergent seam analysis rather than treating cognition as a separate system.
contribution: A structured build-out plan, a wave-synchronous parallel-executor design, a role-registry decision fork, an edge-type analysis, and a shared-vs-divergent seam map that a fused system could use to decide what to keep common and what to split.
summary_for_embedding: A design document planning the build-out of a registry-driven node mechanism to serve both the app surface and a concurrent-cognition layer, with prioritized work items and a shared-vs-divergent seam analysis.
intention_for_embedding: To define the build-out needed to make the node mechanism a dual-use substrate for app and cognition while surfacing where the two uses must share or diverge.
